perturbation.original_text: "Hardware" means the hardware identified on Exhibit A, together with any Updates to such hardware. WGT reserves the right to add to or delete hardware from Exhibit A and to modify the hardware during the Term.
perturbation.changed_text: "Hardware" means the hardware identified on Exhibit A. WGT reserves the right to add to or delete hardware from Exhibit A and to modify the hardware during the Term. Any updates to such hardware shall not be considered part of the original hardware and are subject to separate terms and conditions.
perturbation.explanation: By excluding 'Updates' from the definition of 'Hardware' and stating they are subject to separate terms, the contract introduces ambiguity and potential conflict regarding warranty, support, and liability for hardware updates. It creates uncertainty about whether updates are covered under the original hardware warranty or if they require a separate agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Commercial Code (UCC) - Implied Warranty of Merchantability
perturbation.law_explanation: The UCC implies a warranty of merchantability, meaning goods must be fit for the ordinary purpose for which they are used. By separating 'Updates' from 'Hardware', the modified contract creates a situation where necessary updates might not be covered under the original warranty, potentially violating the implied warranty if the hardware becomes unusable or defective without the updates.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-314. Implied Warranty: Merchantability; Usage of Trade. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-314. Implied Warranty: Merchantability; Usage of Trade. (1) Unless excluded or modified (Section 2-316 ), a warranty that the goods shall be merchantable is implied in a contract for their sale if the seller is a merchant with respect to goods of that kind. Under this section the serving for value of food or drink to be consumed either on the premises or elsewhere is a sale. (2) Goods to be merchantable must be at least such as (a) pass without objection in the trade under the contract description; and (b) in the case of fungible goods , are of fair average quality within the description; and (c) are fit for the ordinary purposes for which such goods are used; and (d) run, within the variations permitted by the agreement , of even kind, quality and quantity within each unit and among all units involved; and (e) are adequately contained, packaged, and labeled as the agreement may require; and (f) conform to the promise or affirmations of fact made on the container or label if any. (3) Unless excluded or modified (Section 2-316 ) other implied warranties may arise from course of dealing or usage of trade. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the UCC's implied warranty of merchantability by separating 'Updates' from 'Hardware.' Snippet 1, § 2-314(2)(c), states goods must be 'fit for the ordinary purposes for which such goods are used.' By excluding updates and making them subject to separate terms, the contract implies the hardware's functionality and warranty might not cover necessary updates, potentially rendering the hardware unfit for its intended purpose without them, thereby creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Subject to and in accordance with the provisions of this Agreement, WGT hereby appoints Distributor, and Distributor hereby accepts WGT's appointment, as a nonexclusive distributor of the Product to resellers in the Territory during the Term, as long as Distributor makes the Guaranteed Minimum Purchases pursuant to Section 4.
perturbation.changed_text: Subject to and in accordance with the provisions of this Agreement, WGT hereby appoints Distributor, and Distributor hereby accepts WGT's appointment, as a distributor of the Product to resellers in the Territory during the Term, regardless of whether Distributor makes the Guaranteed Minimum Purchases pursuant to Section 4.
perturbation.explanation: This change removes the condition that the distributor must make guaranteed minimum purchases, to maintain their status. This is directly contradicting section 4 which outlines the minimum purchase agreement.
perturbation.law_explanation: Consideration is a required part of a legal contract. By removing the requirement for guaranteed minimum purchases, a court could find that this contract lacks consideration, thus voiding it.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: consideration | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you consideration Consideration is a promise , performance, or forbearance bargained by a promisor in exchange for their promise. Consideration is the main element of a contract . Without consideration by both parties, a contract cannot be enforceable. For instance, if a person used the money to purchase an apple, the apple is the merchant’s consideration, and the money is the person’s consideration. Types: Consideration could be a promise, performance, forbearance, or property with legal value, but the economic benefit is not required. A gift or gratuitous promise cannot be a consideration for they have no bargaining. The past performance also cannot be a consideration as there is no exchange. Substitute for consideration: A contract without consideration could be enforceable if it has a substitute. Substitutes are promissory estoppel or detrimental reliance under Restatement (Second) of Contracts , or good faith modification under UCC . Promissory estoppel /detrimental reliance: A contract without consideration is enforceable if the nonperformance of the promisor will cause injustice. Elements of promissory estoppel are (i) the promise has reasonable, foreseeable, and detrimental reliance on the promisor, and (ii) the enforcement of the promise is necessary to avoid injustice. Good faith modification: A modified contract is a kind of new agreement, which changes parties’ obligations and then requires new consideration. But contract modification made in good faith under UCC is enforceable even without consideration. [Last reviewed in July of 2022 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE commercial activities business law contracts wex definitions business sectors commercial transactions legal education and practice Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the 'Guaranteed Minimum Purchases' requirement, which directly contradicts the original contract's condition for maintaining the distributor's appointment. The Law Explanation points out that consideration is a required part of a legal contract, and removing the purchase requirement could be seen as removing consideration. Scraped Law Snippet 2 states, 'Without consideration by both parties, a contract cannot be enforceable.' By removing the minimum purchase requirement, the distributor may no longer be providing adequate consideration, creating a legal contradiction as it undermines the enforceability of the contract. The perturbation type 'Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction' is applicable, as the change deviates from legally recognized definitions of what constitutes a valid, enforceable contract.

perturbation.original_text: WGT will permit Distributor and end-users purchasing through resellers to return any defective Product in accordance with the limited warranty contained in the applicable end-user license agreement or limited hardware warranty, as applicable, provided that the Distributor and end-user have compiled with the applicable warranty terms and conditions. In order to receive the remedy provided for hereunder, Distributor shall deliver to WGT a sample of the Product which Distributor finds to be defective in workmanship or materials, or damaged in shipment prior to Distributor assuming the risk of loss or damage , along with a written explanation of the alleged defect within thirty (30) days from the later of Distributor's initial receipt of such Product from WGT or from the delivery of such Product to an end-user.
perturbation.changed_text: WGT will permit Distributor to return any defective Product, only when WGT approves the refund. In order to receive the remedy provided for hereunder, Distributor shall deliver to WGT a sample of the Product which Distributor finds to be defective in workmanship or materials, or damaged in shipment, along with a written explanation of the alleged defect within ninety (90) days from the later of Distributor's initial receipt of such Product from WGT or from the delivery of such Product to an end-user.
perturbation.explanation: By removing end-users from returning the defective product, the change alters the return policy and might be violating consumer protection laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Magnuson-Moss Warranty Act protects consumers and states the conditions for a warranty. By removing the end-users ability to return defective products directly contradicts this law.

(1/4/1995) Back to Original Document << Previous TITLE 15 / CHAPTER 50 Next >> [Print] [Print selection] [OLRC Home] Help 15 USC Ch. 50 : CONSUMER PRODUCT WARRANTIES From Title 15—COMMERCE AND TRADE CHAPTER 50 —CONSUMER PRODUCT WARRANTIES Sec. 2301. Definitions. 2302. Rules governing contents of warranties. 2303. Designation of written warranties. 2304. Federal minimum standards for warranties. 2305. Full and limited warranting of a consumer product. 2306. Service contracts; rules for full, clear and conspicuous disclosure of terms and conditions; addition to or in lieu of written warranty. 2307. Designation of representatives by warrantor to perform duties under written or implied warranty. 2308. Implied warranties. 2309. Procedures applicable to promulgation of rules by Commission. 2310. Remedies in consumer disputes. 2311. Applicability to other laws. 2312. Effective dates. §2301. Definitions For the purposes of this chapter: (1) The term "consumer product" means any tangible personal property which is distributed in commerce and which is normally used for personal, family, or household purposes (including any such property intended to be attached to or installed in any real property without regard to whether it is so attached or installed). (2) The term "Commission" means the Federal Trade Commission. (3) The term "consumer" means a buyer (other than for purposes of resale) of any consumer product, any person to whom such product is transferred during the duration of an implied or written warranty (or service contract) applicable to the product, and any other person who is entitled by the terms of such warranty (or service contract) or under applicable State law to enforce against the warrantor (or service contractor) the obligations of the warranty (or service contract). (4) The term "supplier" means any person engaged in the business of making a consumer product directly or indirectly available to consumers. (5) The term "warrantor" means any supplier or other person who gives or offers to give a written warranty or who is or may be obligated under an implied warranty. (6) The term "written warranty" means— (A) any written affirmation of fact or written promise made in connection with the sale of a cons
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly allows both the Distributor and end-users to return defective products, aligning with typical consumer protection practices. The changed text removes the end-user's ability to return defective products, limiting returns to only distributors with WGT's approval. The Law Explanation states the Magnuson-Moss Warranty Act protects consumers, and the Perturbation Explanation states the altered text violates consumer protection laws by removing the end-user's ability to return defective products. This aligns with 15 USC Ch. 50, which defines 'consumer' broadly as any buyer or person to whom the product is transferred during the warranty period, indicating a right to enforce warranty obligations. By removing end-users' return rights, the changed contract directly contradicts the consumer protections intended by the law, establishing a legal contradiction as the modified contract removes a legally mandated consumer protection.
